VoLN-UAV Vision-only Language-Model-based Navigation for UAVs
Overview Benchmark Method Results Demo Citation

VoLN-UAV

A Vision-Only Navigation Project Page for UAVs

VoLN replaces the text-instruction interface in conventional VLN with a visual goal interface, enabling UAVs to perform long-horizon closed-loop navigation using only reference images, semantic beacons, and visual cues. During execution, the system does not rely on text tokens, GPS, a global map, or shortest-path information.

Vision-only Goals specified by reference images, beacons, and visual cues
No GPS/Map No map, GPS, or shortest-path access at inference time
Unreal + AirSim High-fidelity UAV simulation and closed-loop evaluation
Long-horizon Focus on long-term memory, re-identification, and stable control
Figure 1. Comparison between conventional VLN and VoLN. VoLN performs closed-loop navigation with a set of visual goals V.

Overview

This project studies vision-only, goal-driven UAV navigation. The paper introduces the VoLN paradigm, the VoLN-UAV benchmark, and an initial method based on DINO→CLIP visual-semantic alignment and LoRA-based trajectory planning.

1. A New Task Paradigm

In VoLN, the navigation goal is no longer specified with natural language. Instead, it is expressed through terminal reference views, key intermediate views, and route-relevant beacons. This makes evaluation closer to real-world settings such as GPS-denied, map-free, and previously unseen environments.

2. A UAV Benchmark Under Realistic Constraints

VoLN-UAV is built on Unreal Engine + Microsoft AirSim and covers desert, forest, urban, tunnel, and industrial-corridor environments. A scene-level split is used to reduce data leakage.

3. Closed-Loop Visual-Semantic Planning

The method first maps DINO visual representations into the CLIP image-semantic space, then constructs discrete visual-semantic tokens. A LoRA-tuned planner finally predicts short-horizon trajectories and control sequences.

Abstract

VLN has made substantial progress in embodied intelligence, enabling agents to follow natural-language instructions in complex environments. However, existing UAV navigation frameworks typically rely heavily on textual instructions, which often implicitly encode privileged information such as global orientation, topological structure, or metric distance. In realistic deployments, especially under GPS-denied, infrastructure-degraded, or safety-critical conditions, such assumptions are often unavailable. In this paper, we propose Vision-only Language-Model-based Navigation (VoLN), which removes episode-level textual instructions and instead expresses navigation intent entirely through visual signals. In VoLN, navigation goals are specified via reference images, semantic beacons, or visually embedded cues, while the agent is forbidden from accessing text tokens, global maps, GPS signals, or shortest-path information during execution. To support this setting, we introduce the VoLN-UAV benchmark for long-horizon goal-directed navigation under realistic perceptual constraints. As an initial instantiation, we propose a vision-driven method that combines visual embeddings, semantic signals, and trajectory generation modules, and further introduce a visual-semantic alignment strategy that maps visual features into a structured semantic space.

Benchmark

VoLN-UAV is built through a pipeline of scene sampling → reference trajectory generation → visual goal interface construction → synchronized multimodal replay → train/validation/test scene splitting.

Environment Pool

The environments include both open-source and custom-built scenes, covering diverse visual and geometric distributions such as desert, forest, urban, tunnel, and industrial-corridor settings.

Construction Pipeline

The visual goal interface consists of terminal goal exemplars, optional subgoals, and optional beacons; during training, future waypoint supervision and beacon annotations are retained.

Execution Inputs

Egocentric RGB + Proprioception + Visual Goal Set V

Strict Constraints

No text, no GPS, no global map, no goal coordinates, no shortest-path info

Difficulty Levels

Easy < 300m，Normal 300–450m，Hard ≥ 450m

Data Split

Scene-level disjoint split to reduce same-scene memorization and repeated-task leakage

Method

The VoLN Navigation MLLM is trained in two stages: visual encoder distillation and semantic alignment first, followed by parameter-efficient trajectory-planning fine-tuning.

DINO → CLIP Alignment + LoRA Planner

Phase I uses a frozen CLIP image encoder as the teacher to align DINO visual representations with the CLIP image-embedding space; Phase II performs LoRA fine-tuning on the planner for short-horizon trajectory prediction and control output.

Core Design

  • Vision tokenization via semantic alignment: Encode observations into aligned visual-semantic embeddings.
  • Offline semantic bank: Use a prebuilt CLIP text semantic bank to generate compact visual-semantic tokens at runtime.
  • Hierarchical decoding: Predict high-level goals / anchors first, then generate executable dense control sequences.
  • Closed-loop prediction: Perform continuous closed-loop navigation under a unified protocol, emphasizing stability and executability.
2-stage Distillation alignment + trajectory fine-tuning
LoRA Parameter-efficient planner adaptation
nDTW / SPL Measure process quality beyond final-goal success

Results

This section keeps only the main comparison results, with values taken from Table 1 in docs/VoLN-UAV-paper.pdf.

Main Results · Test-Unseen (TU)

Method NE (E/N/H) ↓ SR (E/N/H) ↑ OSR (E/N/H) ↑ nDTW (E/N/H) ↑ SPL (E/N/H) ↑
Random 170.1 / 310.4 / 395.2 0.4 / 0.0 / 0.0 1.4 / 0.6 / 0.2 30.1 / – / – 0.3 / – / –
Seq2Seq [33] 208.6 / 254.8 / 309.9 1.0 / 0.4 / 0.1 4.8 / 2.5 / 0.9 28.9 / 21.4 / 13.0 0.7 / 0.3 / 0.0
CMA [34] 174.5 / 216.8 / 266.1 1.6 / 0.8 / 0.2 6.5 / 3.9 / 1.7 33.2 / 26.4 / 18.5 1.1 / 0.6 / 0.1
LAG [16] 122.4 / 158.3 / 206.7 2.3 / 1.2 / 0.4 6.4 / 3.8 / 1.7 28.1 / 20.5 / 14.0 1.5 / 0.7 / 0.2
VoLN-MLLM (Ours) 97.1 / 131.4 / 176.8 7.4 / 4.5 / 1.8 14.6 / 10.1 / 4.5 53.1 / 41.2 / 28.0 5.7 / 3.2 / 1.3

Baseline definitions and references are consistent with the paper: Seq2Seq [33], CMA [34], and LAG [16].
